Într-un mic oraș din sudul țării a fost identificat un român care nu i-a ținut pumnii Alexandrei Căpitănescu în finala Eurovision de sâmbătă seara. El e singurul român care nu i-a ținut pumnii Alexandrei Căpitănescu appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Cocalarii bucureșteni s-au revoltat după ce Primăria a anunțat că vrea să cumpere în jur de 100 de tramvaie noi. Ei spun că și în prezent condițiile de trafic sunt dificile, însă cu încă 100 de tramvaie pe șine ele vor deveni de-a dreptul dezastruoase. „În mod normal ar trebui eliminate din tramvaiele care circulă […] The post Cocalarii cu BMW, revoltați că PMB vrea să cumpere 100 de tramvaie: „Abia avem noi loc pe șine!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

Dis de dimineață, încă mahmuri de supărare că au pierdut, românii au reascultat melodia câștigătoare și și-au adus aminte […] The post Rezultatele Eurovision le-au readus aminte românilor că trebuie să-i urască și pe bulgari appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

medical.title: Open-access tool decodes DNA change patterns in breast cancer
medical.summary: A study led by Dr. Jason Pitt, Principal Investigator at the Cancer Science Institute of Singapore (CSI Singapore), has identified eight new "signatures" of DNA patterns (gains and/or losses) in breast cancer. By analyzing nearly 2,800 genomes, the team systematically profiled changes in the number of DNA copies in breast cancer, with the goal of better understanding the underlying mechanisms of tumor development and evaluating how these structural genomic changes relate to clinical outcomes.

medical.title: How hibernation influences a visual brain area in squirrels
medical.summary: Understanding how hibernation affects neurons sheds light on how neurons adapt to changing states and can inform treatment strategies for conditions in which neurons are damaged or impaired. In a new JNeurosci paper, researchers led by Hendrikje Nienborg, from the National Eye Institute, used squirrels to assess how hibernation alters neuron structure in a brain area that responds to visual information from the eyes. Why focus on this visual brain area?

medical.title: Brain implants allow us to move and talk. But they could also be hacked
medical.summary: The human brain is remarkably complex, with trillions of connections that control how you move, think and feel.

medical.title: A protective gel for a future without insulin injections
medical.summary: Researchers from the University of Geneva (UNIGE) and Geneva University Hospitals (HUG) have reached a significant advance in the fight against type 1 diabetes. Using an innovative hydrogel that supports insulin-producing cells once transplanted into the body, the team successfully regulated blood sugar levels in diabetic mice. This experimental success goes beyond conventional transplantation methods and opens the door to developing a bioartificial pancreas that could eliminate the need for insulin injections. The results are published in the journal Trends in Biotechnology.

medical.title: Why has PCOS been given a new name?
medical.summary: For more than two decades, I have studied a condition that shapes the lives of about 10–13% of women. This condition causes complex, wide-ranging symptoms such as irregular periods, excessive hair growth, weight gain, acne and difficulty getting pregnant.

medical.title: Primary care is seeing more children's mental health needs, led by a surge in anxiety
medical.summary: A new study led by researchers at the Harvard Pilgrim Health Care Institute, Boston University, Boston Medical Center, UMass Chan Medical School, and Brown University finds that children's mental health concerns are appearing more often during routine visits with primary care doctors. Published in JAMA Network Open, the study shows a steady rise over the past decade, with especially large increases in visits related to anxiety.

medical.title: Expert explains the science, safety, and legal landscape around mifepristone used in abortions, miscarriage care
medical.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court has preserved access to mifepristone—the medication used in the majority of abortions in the United States and in some miscarriage care—for now. The action maintains current rules that allow certified clinicians to prescribe the drug through telehealth and pharmacies to dispense it by mail while legal challenges continue.

medical.title: AI-powered CPR coach outperforms 911 dispatchers in guiding bystander resuscitation
medical.summary: A new study from scientists at the University of California San Diego in collaboration with the University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, Johns Hopkins University, and other institutions, demonstrates that an artificial intelligence-powered CPR coaching agent can outperform 911 dispatchers in guiding bystanders through cardiopulmonary resuscitation.

medical.title: Statistical method may overstate Alzheimer's drugs' cognitive benefits by 29 times
medical.summary: A statistical approach being used to support a new class of Alzheimer's drugs may lead to overstated claims about how the drugs work, according to a new study led by researchers at the Brown University School of Public Health.

medical.title: Why an off-label autism drug suddenly took off, despite limited large-scale evidence
medical.summary: Researchers from the University of California San Diego found that prescriptions for leucovorin, a drug sometimes used off-label for autism spectrum disorder (ASD), rose sharply among children after widespread media attention and public statements from White House officials.

medical.title: New drug target identified for Fragile X syndrome
medical.summary: UCLA Health researchers have identified a potential drug target for treating Fragile X syndrome, the most common genetic cause of intellectual disability and autism that affects roughly one in 2,000 boys.

medical.title: Medicare Advantage broker payments potentially hit $10 billion annually, study finds
medical.summary: New research offers one of the clearest pictures yet on just how large the Medicare Advantage insurance broker market has become. Published as a research letter in JAMA Internal Medicine, the study by health policy scholars at the Brown University School of Public Health found that payments to Medicare Advantage brokers more than doubled between 2014 and 2022, rising from about $3.9 billion to roughly $10 billion a year.

medical.title: Project expands access to care for children with amblyopia
medical.summary: A group of pediatric eye disease researchers has launched an open-access tool designed to help manage pediatric cases of amblyopia, a condition in which the brain fails to properly develop normal vision in one or both eyes early in life. It is the leading cause of preventable single-eye (monocular) vision loss, affecting 3 of every 100 children in the nation. The tool is aimed at expanding access to evidence-based amblyopia clinical-decision-making expertise amidst a shortage of pediatric eye care specialists in the United States.

medical.title: This daily habit could lower dementia risk by 35%, scientists say
medical.summary: A huge long-term study found that drinking two to three cups of coffee a day was linked to a much lower risk of dementia, especially before age 75. Researchers say caffeine may help keep brain cells active while reducing inflammation and harmful plaque buildup associated with Alzheimer’s disease. But more coffee wasn’t better — the protective effect appeared to level off after moderate intake.

medical.title: Scientists discover a mysterious silicone pollutant that may be everywhere
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered unexpectedly high levels of silicone-based pollutants called methylsiloxanes floating through the atmosphere across cities, rural regions, and even forests. Much of the pollution appears to come from vehicle emissions, likely linked to engine oil additives that survive combustion and escape into the air. Scientists say humans may inhale more of these compounds daily than other notorious pollutants like PFAS or microplastics.

medical.title: Your “um” and pauses could reveal early dementia risk
medical.summary: The little pauses, “ums,” and moments when you struggle to find the right word may reveal far more about your brain than anyone realized. Researchers discovered that everyday speech patterns are closely tied to executive function — the mental system that powers memory, planning, focus, and flexible thinking. By using AI to analyze natural conversations, the team found they could predict cognitive performance with surprising accuracy, potentially opening the door to simple speech-based tools that could detect early signs of dementia long before traditional testing does.

science.title: Chemical pathway unlocks next-generation infrared III–V nanocrystals
science.summary: A research team led by Professor Sohee Jeong at Sungkyunkwan University has uncovered a key chemical pathway for the controlled synthesis of III–V semiconductor quantum dots, a class of next-generation infrared materials expected to play an important role in autonomous driving sensors, smart sensing systems, night-vision devices, and short-wave infrared optoelectronics.

science.title: Teaching with food boosts preschoolers' science knowledge and vocabulary
science.summary: Using food in the classroom can help preschoolers learn more about science and increase their vocabulary skills, according to new research from North Carolina State University and East Carolina University. It also might get preschoolers to taste, or at least touch, the green vegetables on their dinner plates.

science.title: NASA Selects Next Class of Space Health Postdoctoral Fellows
science.summary: The NASA-funded Translational Research Institute for Space Health (TRISH) has selected two early‑career scientists for its next class of postdoctoral fellows. The new fellows will begin their projects in May, focusing on space food systems and astronaut eye health. The TRISH Postdoctoral Fellowship Program supports independent research that advances biomedical, behavioral, and technological approaches relevant […]

science.title: Eyes that photosynthesize: Scientists plant a cure for dry eye disease
science.summary: What if eyes could use light to heal themselves? Drawing inspiration from how plants harness sunlight, researchers at the National University of Singapore (NUS) are pioneering a revolutionary treatment for dry eye disease. Their approach uses a light-activated technology derived from the photosynthetic membranes of the spinach plant, enabling the eye to stay continuously hydrated. This offers a solution that is simple, effective, and non-invasive.

science.title: When we took 37 strangers into the urban bush, their loneliness began to ease
science.summary: One in three Australians says they are lonely. To be lonely is to feel a lack of adequate social connection. Loneliness is about feeling disconnected from others or unable to form the kinds of relationships people need to feel seen and supported.

science.title: Overfishing hits 11 of 12 Bahamian seafood staples, 73 years of catch data show
science.summary: Most of the Bahamas' signature seafood stocks are being fished harder than the sea can replace them, according to a new paper led by Sea Around Us researchers and published in Frontiers in Marine Science.

science.title: Nature is good for business—and we now have numbers to show it
science.summary: When rivers degrade, pests spread or drought hits crops, nature sends a bill.

science.title: Learning physics can derail some students: New research shows the best way to keep them on track
science.summary: For many undergraduate students, exploring the complexities of physics for the first time, from wading through advanced mathematics, to absorbing information in a large lecture format, can be a daunting endeavor—one that dissuades many students from continuing their studies.

science.title: Thoughtful solar siting can protect ag, biodiversity
science.summary: Researchers have developed a model that identifies prime farmland, habitats critical for biodiversity and areas suitable for solar development in New York, to help communities minimize land-use conflicts when making solar siting decisions.

science.title: AI shapes the design of the electron-ion collider
science.summary: Artificial intelligence and machine learning are shaping major design and research decisions for the planned Electron-Ion Collider (EIC), a next-generation nuclear physics research facility that will collide electrons with protons or nuclei to probe matter's structure.

science.title: Prototype sets record for optical quantum information technology
science.summary: Chinese scientists have developed a programmable quantum computing prototype called Jiuzhang 4.0 that has set a new world record for optical quantum information technology, according to a study published May 13 in the journal Nature.

science.title: Beacon of Light
science.summary: The heart of galaxy M77 shines brightly in this May 7, 2026, image from NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope. The intense glow is due to gas being pulled by the strong gravity of the central black hole into a tight and rapid orbit around it. The motion of the gas causes it to heat up, […]

science.title: Toxic metals in Hudson River striped bass decreased over decades, study shows
science.summary: Striped bass are a popular and often human-consumed commercial and recreational game fish. Trace elements or metals essential for life are in these fish and other eatable fish, as well as nonessential and toxic metals such as mercury, lead, and arsenic. A study led by researchers in the School of Marine and Atmospheric Sciences (SoMAS) at Stony Brook University used fish scales from striped bass to assess the accumulation of toxic metals in these fish. They found that the concentrations of most toxic metals generally reduced over time, yet, with exceptions.

science.title: Understanding pre-service physical education teachers' assessment literacy
science.summary: Not only must teachers design lesson plans to convey information to their students, but they must also develop effective ways to assess their students' knowledge and adjust their teaching practices accordingly. The assessment process looks different for health and physical education teachers compared to their traditional classroom counterparts, and this is where Assistant Professor Xiaolu Liu's research comes into play.

science.title: A smelly dog breath breakthrough: Plant-based spray tackles odor and harmful oral microbes
science.summary: Pet owners love their dogs but may not always love the smell of their breath. Because this bad odor can signal oral disease, veterinary clinics will prescribe daily toothbrushing, antibiotics, or chemical rinses as treatment. Now, researchers reporting in the Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry propose an alternative: polyphenols from molasses. They developed a spray that reduced stinky breath and harmful oral bacteria in dogs.

science.title: How wasted infrared light could boost solar panels, night vision and 3D printing
science.summary: Researchers at UNSW Sydney have developed a nanoscale device that converts low-energy infrared and red light into higher-energy visible light, a breakthrough that could eventually improve solar panels, sensing technologies, and advanced manufacturing systems.

science.title: Scientists finally solve the 100-year mystery behind tough tires
science.summary: For nearly 100 years, reinforced rubber has powered everything from car tires to airplanes, yet scientists never fully understood why adding tiny particles of carbon black made rubber so incredibly strong. Now, researchers at the University of South Florida have finally cracked the mystery using massive computer simulations that took the equivalent of 15 years of computing time. They discovered that carbon black forces rubber to “fight against itself” when stretched, dramatically boosting its strength and durability.

science.title: Quantum breakthrough could revolutionize teleportation and computing
science.summary: Scientists in Japan have developed a new way to instantly detect elusive quantum “W states,” a major milestone for quantum technology. The breakthrough could help unlock faster quantum communication, teleportation, and powerful new computing systems.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Asian Food Fest are loc între 22 și 24 mai 2026, in Parcul Titan
mix_items.summary: Minivacanță culinară în Asia: de la colțunași chinezești la copane de înghețată Asia Food Fest are loc între 22 și 24 mai, în Parcul Titan Între 22 și 24 mai… Articolul Asian Food Fest are loc între 22 și 24 mai 2026, in Parcul Titan apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: După ce anul trecut, prin „Susține un copil să meargă la teatru gratuit”, 760 de copii au ajuns la spectacolele Tzitzi-Poc în 10 orașe din țară, campania merge mai departe… Articolul Asociația Tzitzi-Poc continuă campania „Susține un copil să meargă la teatru gratuit”, prin care oferă acces la spectacole copiilor din medii vulnerabile apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Peste 200 de activități și 1800 de bârlădeni implicați: rezultatele celor 6 inițiative finanțate în ediția a 6-a a „Fondului pentru un viitor mai bun în comunități”, derulat de Fundația Comunitară Bacău cu sprijinul Lidl România
mix_items.summary: Copiii și tinerii din Bârlad au avut, în ultimele luni, acces la experiențe de învățare diverse, creative și incluzive, prin 6 inițiative educaționale finanțate prin ediția a 6-a a programului… Articolul Peste 200 de activități și 1800 de bârlădeni implicați: rezultatele celor 6 inițiative finanțate în ediția a 6-a a „Fondului pentru un viitor mai bun în comunități”, derulat de Fundația Comunitară Bacău cu sprijinul Lidl România apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Pavilionul României la Bienala de Artă de la Veneția s-a deschis cu proiectul Anca Benera & Arnold Estefán: Black Seas – Scores for the Sonic Eye / Marea Neagră la Plural – Compoziții pentru ochiul sonor.
mix_items.summary: Bienala de la Veneția a debutat în acest an cu un program dedicat profesioniștilor și presei, în perioada 6-8 mai 2026. La Pavilionul României au avut loc un performance sonor… Articolul Pavilionul României la Bienala de Artă de la Veneția s-a deschis cu proiectul Anca Benera & Arnold Estefán: Black Seas – Scores for the Sonic Eye / Marea Neagră la Plural – Compoziții pentru ochiul sonor. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Cocalarii cu BMW, revoltați că PMB vrea să cumpere 100 de tramvaie: „Abia avem noi loc pe șine!”
mix_items.summary: Cocalarii bucureșteni s-au revoltat după ce Primăria a anunțat că vrea să cumpere în jur de 100 de tramvaie noi. Ei spun că și în prezent condițiile de trafic sunt dificile, însă cu încă 100 de tramvaie pe șine ele vor deveni de-a dreptul dezastruoase. „În mod normal ar trebui eliminate din tramvaiele care circulă […] The post Cocalarii cu BMW, revoltați că PMB vrea să cumpere 100 de tramvaie: „Abia avem noi loc pe șine!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Urban Playfield ajunge la Oradea cu două zile de mișcare, dans și sport în Piața Unirii
mix_items.summary: Urban Playfield, festivalul sporturilor luate în joacă, ajunge la Oradea! În weekendul 23–24 mai, Piața Unirii din oraș va fi transformată în teren de joacă pentru mișcare, dans, demonstrații sportive… Articolul Urban Playfield ajunge la Oradea cu două zile de mișcare, dans și sport în Piața Unirii apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Rubik Hub invită comunitatea de startup la evenimentul Mix to Match, în Iași
mix_items.summary: Pe 26 mai, Rubik Hub invită comunitatea de startup la evenimentul Mix to Match, organizat în Iași, cu ocazia aniversării a 9 ani de activitate. Este prilejul în care cei… Articolul Rubik Hub invită comunitatea de startup la evenimentul Mix to Match, în Iași apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: O veste șocantă s-a abătut azi asupra țării noastre! Într-un mic oraș din sudul țării a fost identificat un român care nu i-a ținut pumnii Alexandrei Căpitănescu în finala Eurovision de sâmbătă seara. Pentru a-l proteja de un eventual linșaj din partea românilor furioși, locația sa exactă nu a fost (și nici nu va fi) […] The post Bestia cu chip uman! El e singurul român care nu i-a ținut pumnii Alexandrei Căpitănescu appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Rezultatele Eurovision le-au readus aminte românilor că trebuie să-i urască și pe bulgari
mix_items.summary: Ca aproape în fiecare an România a fost văduvită de marele premiu de la Eurovision în urma unor evidente jocuri de culise, mișcări strategie europene și a trădării neașteptate a unor aliați pe care ne bazam. Dis de dimineață, încă mahmuri de supărare că au pierdut, românii au reascultat melodia câștigătoare și și-au adus aminte […] The post Rezultatele Eurovision le-au readus aminte românilor că trebuie să-i urască și pe bulgari appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Chemical pathway unlocks next-generation infrared III–V nanocrystals
mix_items.summary: A research team led by Professor Sohee Jeong at Sungkyunkwan University has uncovered a key chemical pathway for the controlled synthesis of III–V semiconductor quantum dots, a class of next-generation infrared materials expected to play an important role in autonomous driving sensors, smart sensing systems, night-vision devices, and short-wave infrared optoelectronics.

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Teaching with food boosts preschoolers' science knowledge and vocabulary
mix_items.summary: Using food in the classroom can help preschoolers learn more about science and increase their vocabulary skills, according to new research from North Carolina State University and East Carolina University. It also might get preschoolers to taste, or at least touch, the green vegetables on their dinner plates.

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Open-access tool decodes DNA change patterns in breast cancer
mix_items.summary: A study led by Dr. Jason Pitt, Principal Investigator at the Cancer Science Institute of Singapore (CSI Singapore), has identified eight new "signatures" of DNA patterns (gains and/or losses) in breast cancer. By analyzing nearly 2,800 genomes, the team systematically profiled changes in the number of DNA copies in breast cancer, with the goal of better understanding the underlying mechanisms of tumor development and evaluating how these structural genomic changes relate to clinical outcomes.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: NASA Selects Next Class of Space Health Postdoctoral Fellows
mix_items.summary: The NASA-funded Translational Research Institute for Space Health (TRISH) has selected two early‑career scientists for its next class of postdoctoral fellows. The new fellows will begin their projects in May, focusing on space food systems and astronaut eye health. The TRISH Postdoctoral Fellowship Program supports independent research that advances biomedical, behavioral, and technological approaches relevant […]

mix_items.title: How hibernation influences a visual brain area in squirrels
mix_items.summary: Understanding how hibernation affects neurons sheds light on how neurons adapt to changing states and can inform treatment strategies for conditions in which neurons are damaged or impaired. In a new JNeurosci paper, researchers led by Hendrikje Nienborg, from the National Eye Institute, used squirrels to assess how hibernation alters neuron structure in a brain area that responds to visual information from the eyes. Why focus on this visual brain area?

mix_items.title: Eyes that photosynthesize: Scientists plant a cure for dry eye disease
mix_items.summary: What if eyes could use light to heal themselves? Drawing inspiration from how plants harness sunlight, researchers at the National University of Singapore (NUS) are pioneering a revolutionary treatment for dry eye disease. Their approach uses a light-activated technology derived from the photosynthetic membranes of the spinach plant, enabling the eye to stay continuously hydrated. This offers a solution that is simple, effective, and non-invasive.

mix_items.title: When we took 37 strangers into the urban bush, their loneliness began to ease
mix_items.summary: One in three Australians says they are lonely. To be lonely is to feel a lack of adequate social connection. Loneliness is about feeling disconnected from others or unable to form the kinds of relationships people need to feel seen and supported.

mix_items.title: Overfishing hits 11 of 12 Bahamian seafood staples, 73 years of catch data show
mix_items.summary: Most of the Bahamas' signature seafood stocks are being fished harder than the sea can replace them, according to a new paper led by Sea Around Us researchers and published in Frontiers in Marine Science.

mix_items.title: Nature is good for business—and we now have numbers to show it
mix_items.summary: When rivers degrade, pests spread or drought hits crops, nature sends a bill.

mix_items.title: Brain implants allow us to move and talk. But they could also be hacked
mix_items.summary: The human brain is remarkably complex, with trillions of connections that control how you move, think and feel.

mix_items.title: Learning physics can derail some students: New research shows the best way to keep them on track
mix_items.summary: For many undergraduate students, exploring the complexities of physics for the first time, from wading through advanced mathematics, to absorbing information in a large lecture format, can be a daunting endeavor—one that dissuades many students from continuing their studies.

mix_items.title: Thoughtful solar siting can protect ag, biodiversity
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed a model that identifies prime farmland, habitats critical for biodiversity and areas suitable for solar development in New York, to help communities minimize land-use conflicts when making solar siting decisions.

mix_items.title: AI shapes the design of the electron-ion collider
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence and machine learning are shaping major design and research decisions for the planned Electron-Ion Collider (EIC), a next-generation nuclear physics research facility that will collide electrons with protons or nuclei to probe matter's structure.

mix_items.title: Prototype sets record for optical quantum information technology
mix_items.summary: Chinese scientists have developed a programmable quantum computing prototype called Jiuzhang 4.0 that has set a new world record for optical quantum information technology, according to a study published May 13 in the journal Nature.

mix_items.title: A protective gel for a future without insulin injections
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the University of Geneva (UNIGE) and Geneva University Hospitals (HUG) have reached a significant advance in the fight against type 1 diabetes. Using an innovative hydrogel that supports insulin-producing cells once transplanted into the body, the team successfully regulated blood sugar levels in diabetic mice. This experimental success goes beyond conventional transplantation methods and opens the door to developing a bioartificial pancreas that could eliminate the need for insulin injections. The results are published in the journal Trends in Biotechnology.

mix_items.title: Beacon of Light
mix_items.summary: The heart of galaxy M77 shines brightly in this May 7, 2026, image from NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope. The intense glow is due to gas being pulled by the strong gravity of the central black hole into a tight and rapid orbit around it. The motion of the gas causes it to heat up, […]

mix_items.title: Why has PCOS been given a new name?
mix_items.summary: For more than two decades, I have studied a condition that shapes the lives of about 10–13% of women. This condition causes complex, wide-ranging symptoms such as irregular periods, excessive hair growth, weight gain, acne and difficulty getting pregnant.

mix_items.title: Toxic metals in Hudson River striped bass decreased over decades, study shows
mix_items.summary: Striped bass are a popular and often human-consumed commercial and recreational game fish. Trace elements or metals essential for life are in these fish and other eatable fish, as well as nonessential and toxic metals such as mercury, lead, and arsenic. A study led by researchers in the School of Marine and Atmospheric Sciences (SoMAS) at Stony Brook University used fish scales from striped bass to assess the accumulation of toxic metals in these fish. They found that the concentrations of most toxic metals generally reduced over time, yet, with exceptions.

mix_items.title: Understanding pre-service physical education teachers' assessment literacy
mix_items.summary: Not only must teachers design lesson plans to convey information to their students, but they must also develop effective ways to assess their students' knowledge and adjust their teaching practices accordingly. The assessment process looks different for health and physical education teachers compared to their traditional classroom counterparts, and this is where Assistant Professor Xiaolu Liu's research comes into play.

mix_items.title: A smelly dog breath breakthrough: Plant-based spray tackles odor and harmful oral microbes
mix_items.summary: Pet owners love their dogs but may not always love the smell of their breath. Because this bad odor can signal oral disease, veterinary clinics will prescribe daily toothbrushing, antibiotics, or chemical rinses as treatment. Now, researchers reporting in the Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry propose an alternative: polyphenols from molasses. They developed a spray that reduced stinky breath and harmful oral bacteria in dogs.

mix_items.title: Primary care is seeing more children's mental health needs, led by a surge in anxiety
mix_items.summary: A new study led by researchers at the Harvard Pilgrim Health Care Institute, Boston University, Boston Medical Center, UMass Chan Medical School, and Brown University finds that children's mental health concerns are appearing more often during routine visits with primary care doctors. Published in JAMA Network Open, the study shows a steady rise over the past decade, with especially large increases in visits related to anxiety.

mix_items.title: Expert explains the science, safety, and legal landscape around mifepristone used in abortions, miscarriage care
mix_items.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court has preserved access to mifepristone—the medication used in the majority of abortions in the United States and in some miscarriage care—for now. The action maintains current rules that allow certified clinicians to prescribe the drug through telehealth and pharmacies to dispense it by mail while legal challenges continue.

mix_items.title: AI-powered CPR coach outperforms 911 dispatchers in guiding bystander resuscitation
mix_items.summary: A new study from scientists at the University of California San Diego in collaboration with the University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, Johns Hopkins University, and other institutions, demonstrates that an artificial intelligence-powered CPR coaching agent can outperform 911 dispatchers in guiding bystanders through cardiopulmonary resuscitation.

mix_items.title: Statistical method may overstate Alzheimer's drugs' cognitive benefits by 29 times
mix_items.summary: A statistical approach being used to support a new class of Alzheimer's drugs may lead to overstated claims about how the drugs work, according to a new study led by researchers at the Brown University School of Public Health.

mix_items.title: Why an off-label autism drug suddenly took off, despite limited large-scale evidence
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the University of California San Diego found that prescriptions for leucovorin, a drug sometimes used off-label for autism spectrum disorder (ASD), rose sharply among children after widespread media attention and public statements from White House officials.

mix_items.title: New drug target identified for Fragile X syndrome
mix_items.summary: UCLA Health researchers have identified a potential drug target for treating Fragile X syndrome, the most common genetic cause of intellectual disability and autism that affects roughly one in 2,000 boys.

mix_items.title: Medicare Advantage broker payments potentially hit $10 billion annually, study finds
mix_items.summary: New research offers one of the clearest pictures yet on just how large the Medicare Advantage insurance broker market has become. Published as a research letter in JAMA Internal Medicine, the study by health policy scholars at the Brown University School of Public Health found that payments to Medicare Advantage brokers more than doubled between 2014 and 2022, rising from about $3.9 billion to roughly $10 billion a year.

mix_items.title: Project expands access to care for children with amblyopia
mix_items.summary: A group of pediatric eye disease researchers has launched an open-access tool designed to help manage pediatric cases of amblyopia, a condition in which the brain fails to properly develop normal vision in one or both eyes early in life. It is the leading cause of preventable single-eye (monocular) vision loss, affecting 3 of every 100 children in the nation. The tool is aimed at expanding access to evidence-based amblyopia clinical-decision-making expertise amidst a shortage of pediatric eye care specialists in the United States.

mix_items.title: How wasted infrared light could boost solar panels, night vision and 3D printing
mix_items.summary: Researchers at UNSW Sydney have developed a nanoscale device that converts low-energy infrared and red light into higher-energy visible light, a breakthrough that could eventually improve solar panels, sensing technologies, and advanced manufacturing systems.

mix_items.title: This daily habit could lower dementia risk by 35%, scientists say
mix_items.summary: A huge long-term study found that drinking two to three cups of coffee a day was linked to a much lower risk of dementia, especially before age 75. Researchers say caffeine may help keep brain cells active while reducing inflammation and harmful plaque buildup associated with Alzheimer’s disease. But more coffee wasn’t better — the protective effect appeared to level off after moderate intake.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the 100-year mystery behind tough tires
mix_items.summary: For nearly 100 years, reinforced rubber has powered everything from car tires to airplanes, yet scientists never fully understood why adding tiny particles of carbon black made rubber so incredibly strong. Now, researchers at the University of South Florida have finally cracked the mystery using massive computer simulations that took the equivalent of 15 years of computing time. They discovered that carbon black forces rubber to “fight against itself” when stretched, dramatically boosting its strength and durability.

mix_items.title: Quantum breakthrough could revolutionize teleportation and computing
mix_items.summary: Scientists in Japan have developed a new way to instantly detect elusive quantum “W states,” a major milestone for quantum technology. The breakthrough could help unlock faster quantum communication, teleportation, and powerful new computing systems.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a mysterious silicone pollutant that may be everywhere
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered unexpectedly high levels of silicone-based pollutants called methylsiloxanes floating through the atmosphere across cities, rural regions, and even forests. Much of the pollution appears to come from vehicle emissions, likely linked to engine oil additives that survive combustion and escape into the air. Scientists say humans may inhale more of these compounds daily than other notorious pollutants like PFAS or microplastics.

mix_items.title: Your “um” and pauses could reveal early dementia risk
mix_items.summary: The little pauses, “ums,” and moments when you struggle to find the right word may reveal far more about your brain than anyone realized. Researchers discovered that everyday speech patterns are closely tied to executive function — the mental system that powers memory, planning, focus, and flexible thinking. By using AI to analyze natural conversations, the team found they could predict cognitive performance with surprising accuracy, potentially opening the door to simple speech-based tools that could detect early signs of dementia long before traditional testing does.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce nivel de motivatie ai azi? - Decorativ.

satire_ro.title: Bestia cu chip uman! El e singurul român care nu i-a ținut pumnii Alexandrei Căpitănescu
